title: How to Keep Your Job During Kundalini Crisis

body:
How to Keep Your Job During Kundalini Crisis

You're reading this at 3 AM, aren't you? Your heart's pounding, your mind's racing, and you're probably wondering how you're going to make it through another workday. You've got this wild energy tearing through you, making you question everything, and the thought of showing up to your job and pretending you're fine? It feels impossible. I get it. I’ve been there. That feeling of being totally alone, like no one else could possibly understand what's happening to you, while the world just keeps spinning like normal. It's a special kind of hell.

You’re not crazy. You're not broken. You're just in the middle of something incredibly intense, something that most people won't ever experience. And the good news - or maybe the bad news, depending on how you look at it - is that you still have to pay your bills. You still have to function. So let's talk about how you keep your job when your inner world feels like it's exploding.

Accept What’s Happening (As Much As You Can)

First off, stop fighting it so hard. I know, easier said than done, right? But the more you resist this energy, this process, the more chaotic it feels. You’re in a spiritual emergency. Your system is rebooting, upgrading, whatever you want to call it. It's not a breakdown, it's a breakthrough, even if it feels like a total demolition right now. Understanding this, even just a little bit, can give you a tiny bit of peace. You’re not falling apart; you’re being put back together differently. It’s messy, it’s terrifying, but it’s happening. And it’s not going to stop just because you have a TPS report due.

Look, you're not going to be able to explain this to your boss. You're not going to be able to explain it to most of your friends, or even your family. They'd look at you like you've lost your mind. And frankly, you might feel like you have. But you haven't. You're experiencing something that's been documented for centuries, just not in a way that fits into our modern, Western, medical model. If you're looking for some validation, some proof that you're not alone, books like Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) can be a lifeline. They'll tell you that what you're going through has a name, and that other people have survived it. That alone can be a huge comfort when you're feeling utterly isolated.

Keep it Professional, Keep it Private

This is crucial. You might feel like you’re screaming inside, but at work, you have to keep a lid on it. You can't go into the office and tell your colleagues that you saw flashing lights behind your eyes all night, or that you felt electricity shooting up your spine, or that you're hearing voices. They won't get it. They'll get scared. And then you'll lose your job. It's that simple.

So, you create a persona. You put on your "I'm a normal, functioning human being" mask. It’s exhausting, I know. It takes every ounce of energy you have. But it’s necessary. You smile, you nod, you do your work. You keep your head down. You don't volunteer for extra projects. You don't try to be the office superstar. Your only goal right now is survival. Just show up, do the bare minimum to get by, and leave.

If you absolutely must explain something - maybe you're having trouble focusing, or you're more irritable than usual - frame it in terms that people understand. "I'm dealing with some personal stress right now." "I haven't been sleeping well." "I'm not feeling 100%." These are vague enough that they don't invite questions, but they offer a plausible explanation for any slight dip in your performance or mood. Don't elaborate. Don't make eye contact for too long. Just get the words out and move on.

Manage Your Energy (What Little You Have)

Kundalini can be incredibly draining, even when it feels like a surge of pure energy. It’s like your body is running a marathon 24/7. So, you have to conserve what you've got. This means:

  • Sleep: Get as much as you can, whenever you can. Even if it's just an hour or two. Don't fight the urge to rest. Your body is doing a tremendous amount of internal work.
  • Food: Eat simple, nourishing food. Your digestion might be weird. Stick to things that are easy on your system. Avoid junk, sugar, caffeine (if it makes you more wired), and alcohol (it'll just make things worse).
  • Movement: Gentle movement. Walking, stretching, yoga if you can manage it. Nothing too intense. You're not trying to push your body; you're trying to help it integrate.
  • Boundaries: Say NO to everything you possibly can outside of work. No social engagements, no extra commitments. Your energy is a precious resource. Guard it fiercely.

You might find yourself needing to take frequent breaks at work, just to collect yourself. Go to the bathroom, splash some water on your face, do a quick grounding exercise. Imagine roots growing from your feet into the earth. Breathe deeply. Just a few minutes can make a difference. Know what I mean? You’re not going to be able to meditate your way out of this at your desk, but you can give yourself tiny moments of respite.

Find Your Support System (Outside of Work)

You need someone to talk to. Someone who won't judge you, someone who can just listen. It's rare, but these people exist. It might be a therapist who's open to spiritual experiences, or a spiritual teacher, or even just a very trusted friend who's seen you through some weird stuff before. Just having one person you can be completely honest with can be a lifesaver. This is not the time to be a lone wolf.

There are also online communities, forums, and groups dedicated to kundalini awakening. Be careful, though. Some are full of well-meaning but misguided people, or even those who might exploit your vulnerability. But others can be incredibly supportive. Just use your discernment. You're already sensitive as hell, so trust your gut about who you connect with.

Books can be a form of support too. Reading about others' experiences can make you feel less alone. The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) is a classic for a reason - it documents many different experiences, showing you the wide range of what's possible. And The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) offers practical advice for navigating the process. These aren't cures, but they're maps. And a map can be everything when you feel lost in the wilderness.

Consider Accommodations (If You Absolutely Must)

This is a tricky one, and I'd advise against it unless you're truly desperate and your job is on the line. In some places, you might be able to request accommodations under disability laws if you can get a doctor to classify your condition as a mental health issue. But here's the thing: that puts a label on you. And once that label is there, it's hard to get rid of. It also means you have to disclose sensitive information, and you open yourself up to potential discrimination, even if it's illegal.

If you go this route, you'd need a doctor who is sympathetic and understands the seriousness of your condition. Most doctors won't. They'll just prescribe you meds that might dull the experience, but won't stop it, and might make you feel even worse. So, this is a last resort, and one you need to think through very carefully. Right? You don't want to dig yourself into a deeper hole.

A better option, if possible, is to take a leave of absence - FMLA, short-term disability, whatever your company offers. Again, you'd likely need a doctor's note, but you might be able to frame it as "severe anxiety" or "burnout" without going into the specifics of kundalini. This gives you time and space to deal with what's happening without the pressure of work. It's not always an option, especially if you can't afford to be without income, but if it is, consider it seriously.

Grounding, Grounding, Grounding

When you're feeling unmoored, like you're floating outside your body or completely disconnected from reality, grounding is your best friend. And it's something you can do discreetly at work.

  • Feel your feet on the floor: Really focus on the sensation. The pressure, the texture of your shoes.
  • Touch something real: Your desk, your pen, your mug. Feel its weight, its temperature.
  • Focus on your breath: Deep breaths into your belly. Not just shallow chest breathing. In through the nose, out through the mouth.
  • Engage your senses: What do you smell? What do you hear? What do you see around you that's ordinary and mundane? The more you bring yourself into the present moment, the less your mind can run wild.

This isn't about stopping the kundalini, it's about giving yourself a stable anchor while it does its work. It's like being in a hurricane and holding onto a tree. You're still in the storm, but you're not getting swept away. Does that land? It's about finding those small moments of stability in the chaos.

Remember Your "Why"

Why are you doing this? Why are you fighting so hard to keep this job? Is it for money? For insurance? For a sense of normalcy? Whatever it is, hold onto that. When you feel like you can't go on, when the energy is overwhelming and your job feels meaningless, remember that practical reason. It's a tether to the real world, a reason to push through the next hour, the next meeting, the next email.

This isn't forever. It feels like it now, I know. But it won't last at this intensity forever. You will integrate this. You will find a new normal. It might be a different normal, a weird normal, but it will be yours. And you'll be stronger for it, even if you can't see that now.

So yeah, it's rough. It's the hardest thing you'll ever go through. But you're not alone, and you can get through this. You can keep your job. You just have to be smart, be quiet, and be incredibly kind to yourself.

And if things get really, really dark, if you feel like you're losing your grip entirely, please, please reach out for help. Books like The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) talk about the thin line between spiritual emergency and psychological crisis. It's a real thing, and sometimes you need professional help to walk that line safely.

You're a warrior. You just don't know it yet. Now go get some rest, even if it's just for a few minutes. You've earned it.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
